script_system_append: Cast the rider as a heavy-set or plus-size American senior age 55+ and keep the same rider across connected shots. Use real-life lifestyle scenes only. Follow the framing logic seen in c1 and c2: the seated rider and wheelchair should feel full-sized, grounded, and naturally proportioned, never toy-sized or oversized. The rider should visibly fill the seat with natural support, but the torso must not tower unrealistically above the chair.
script_user_append: Keep the ad in seamless real-world B-roll with simple high-success actions only: calm driving, steady seated posture, warm walk-and-talk beside the chair, direct smile, or thumbs-up. Build a clear connected route across the three scenes, such as back doorway or patio -> garden path -> quiet neighborhood sidewalk or lane, so the edit feels like one believable outing instead of repeated versions of the same shot. Leave clean negative space for floating UI overlays such as $599, 500W dual motors, 22-inch seat, 330 LBS, FDA 510(k), free returns, and PayPal financing. Do not generate any baked-in captions, signage text, floating words, or logo text inside the footage itself.
scene_pic_system_append: Use the same wheelchair and rider identity in every connected shot. Match the visual proportion cues from c1 and c2: medium or medium-wide portrait framing, most of the seated body and most of the wheelchair visible, minimal empty headroom, and a substantial 22-inch seat that visibly supports a plus-size senior naturally. Do not let the wheelchair read tiny under the rider, and do not let the rider read shrunken inside an oversized chair.
scene_pic_user_append: Keep the chair in normal open riding position only. The rider should read as a heavy-set or plus-size American senior 55+ with a broad torso, fuller thighs, and a calm confident expression. The seat should look wide and supportive, with the rider occupying it naturally like c1 and c2. Use front-side or joystick-side angles only, and avoid white-background packshot energy. Keep the rear/top-back structure compact and realistic: do not invent extra rods, poles, antenna-like parts, cane-like extensions, or exaggerated push bars behind the backrest. No folded-chair action, no hand-folding action, no extreme wheel close-up, and no awkward anatomy. During self-operated motion, the right hand must visibly rest on the joystick.
video_prompt_append: Preserve c1 and c2 proportion logic in motion: the rider and wheelchair must move as one believable full-size unit. Keep the rider heavy-set and well-supported by the chair, with the 22-inch seat reading broad and stable, not cartoonishly huge or tiny. Keep the rear/top-back structure compact and realistic, with no extra rods, poles, antenna-like parts, cane-like extensions, or exaggerated push bars behind the backrest. Frame the subject like a Meta ad: medium or medium-wide 9:16 lifestyle composition, realistic background, simple successful action, clean negative space for UI overlays, and a strong sales finish without turning the video into a product packshot. The route should feel continuous from shot to shot, not like three repeated versions of the same path. If spoken narration is requested in the scene details, keep that ad narration active through most of the clip and avoid long silent stretches. The generated footage itself must contain no readable letters, no built-in subtitles, no fake UI text, and no gibberish text artifacts.
